revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鎮魂祭]]





{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated|qq=}}

{{complex|qq=}}
'''Chinkon-sai''' is an ancient Japanese ritual intended to stop the soul of someone from leaving their body. It comes from the belief that if you stop the person's soul from leaving their body then it will prevcent their death. As an imperial ritual it is done for the Emperor to avoid his death. This ceremony, detailed in the Commentary on the Legal Code (Ryō no gige), served to reinforce the spiritual strength of the emperor before he performed significant rituals like {{ill|Daijosai|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijosai|qq=}} and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]].<ref>{{Cite web|date=2023-09-29|title=Chinkon-sai {{!|qq=}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230929235630/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9291|access-date=2024-04-26|website=web.archive.org|qq=}}</ref> It may have been inseparable from {{ill|Daijosai|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijosai|qq=}} in ancient times.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last=Matsumae|first=Takeshi|date=1980|title=The Heavenly Rock-Grotto Myth and the Chinkon Ceremony|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1178068|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=39|issue=2|pages=9–22|doi=10.2307/1178068|issn=0385-2342|qq=}}</ref>
As a state ritual under the Ritsuryō system, Chinkon-sai was scheduled on the "day of the lion" (tora) before these major rites. The first mention of this ritual occurs in the eleventh month of 685 in the Chronicles of Japan (Nihon shoki). It was typically conducted within the Imperial Household Ministry (Kunaishō), where a "seat" (kamiza) for the deity was prepared. Shrine virgins (mikannagi) and {{ill|kagura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kagura|qq=}}-dancers ({{ill|Sarume clan|lt=Sarume|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|en:draft|6=ja|7=猿女君|8=fr|9=Clan Sarume|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}) from the [[Department of Divinities]] (Jingikan) performed the ceremony, with ministers and lesser officials attending and bearing the emperor's garments. A large vessel known as ukifunetsuki, used in the ceremony, is linked to the legend of the Heavenly Rock Cave (Ama no Iwato), though interpretations of its significance vary.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Matsumae|first=Takeshi|date=1980|title=The Heavenly Rock-Grotto Myth and the Chinkon Ceremony|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1178068|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=39|issue=2|pages=9–22|doi=10.2307/1178068|issn=0385-2342|qq=}}</ref>
Despite the Imperial Household Ministry buildings ceasing to exist after the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, Chinkon-sai continued to be observed at the original site. The festival was abolished in the fifteenth century, but it was later revived in pre-modern times, albeit with changes to the traditional formula. Since the Meiji era, Chinkon-sai has been performed within the imperial palace. In the ancient and medieval eras, similar festivals for the pacification of the spirit were conducted for junior empresses and the crown prince.<ref name=":0" />
==See Also==
* {{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10297557}}
* not to be confused with the [[Fire-Pacification Festival|Fire Calming Festival]] or Chinka-sai
* {{ill|Shōkonsai (Spirit-Summoning Festival)|ja|招魂祭|1=Shōkonsai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsai (Spirit-Summoning Festival)|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsai|qq=}}
